role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1111 Management Analysts 15.5% of wage bill + 13-1199 Business Operations Specialists 2.9% (4-digit 5416 proxy); the real core of this catch-all sits in the unlisted tail — energy consultants/engineers, economists, agronomists, safety professionals, scientists — est. ~20-30% more
role_hints.note: Modeling, benchmarking, expert-report drafting, literature synthesis and compliance documentation are heavily AI-exposed; testimony, field agronomy/safety inspection, and client-specific judgment persist. Within-role fraction depends strongly on the dominant sub-segment — justify from its task mix.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 13.5% (proxy) + tail managers est. ~4% more
role_hints.note: 21,931 of 26,288 firms have <5 employees — substantially owner-operator compensation; expert reputation and relationships persist.
Reporting and tracking automate; client/agency coordination persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Representatives of Services 3.6% + 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 1.4% (proxy)
role_hints.note: Proposal production automates; expert-referral selling does not.
Energy modeling and economic analysis tooling; AI-augmented.
Invoicing, scheduling, document production — directly automatable overhead.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10; crop consultants, safety inspectors, field scientists in the tail, est. ~5-10%
role_hints.note: Physical presence — field scouting, site safety audits, sampling. Sensor/drone-augmented but not replaced.

source_hints.source: Census Business Trends and Outlook Survey (BTOS) AI supplement
source_hints.why: Official AI-use rates for professional/scientific/technical services — the most defensible adoption anchor for a catch-all with no dedicated survey.
Deal counts and EV/EBITDA multiples across niche technical consulting segments, including energy and ESG-adjacent advisory.
SDE-basis sale multiples for the micro tier (83% of firms <5 employees).

source_hints.source: NAICC (National Alliance of Independent Crop Consultants) / ASFMRA member data
source_hints.why: If independent crop consulting proves a material sub-segment, these associations frame its structure and demographics; confirm they publish usable survey data.

source_hints.source: Association of Energy Engineers (AEE) / AESP surveys
source_hints.why: If energy consulting is the dominant sub-segment, these associations cover the practitioner base (CEM certification rolls) and technology adoption; confirm survey products exist.

source_hints.source: Exit Planning Institute State of Owner Readiness surveys
source_hints.why: Owner-age/succession context for micro professional-services firms.

capture_questions: Fee mechanisms vary by sub-segment — hourly expert rates (economic/litigation consulting), per-acre or seasonal retainers (crop consulting), project fees and shared-savings deals (energy consulting): for the dominant sub-segment, name the mechanism and determine who keeps AI savings under it — the operator as margin, or the client via rescoped hours/competitive bids?
Who owns the client relationship — the firm, or the named expert whose reputation IS the product (especially in litigation/economic and specialized scientific work)? What survives an acquisition?

What stops clients from using generic AI for the analytical core (energy audits from utility data, economic modeling, agronomic recommendations from field data) — is the moat credentials, liability, proprietary data, or physical field presence?
Is revenue recurring (retained monitoring, seasonal programs, compliance calendars) or one-shot expert projects, and does AI shorten the projects it doesn't eliminate?
terminal_value_question: For the dominant sub-segment, name the mechanism that would keep paid demand alive under mature AI — a credential courts/regulators/insurers require (expert testimony admissibility, certified energy auditor requirements in some utility programs), a physical act (field scouting, site inspection), or proprietary data — and test whether that mechanism actually binds: if the product is fundamentally analysis of client-supplied data, mature AI replaces the product itself, not just the operator's cost of producing it.
special_notes: CATCH-ALL CODE: 541690 is 'Other Scientific and Technical Consulting' — a residual spanning energy consulting, economic consulting, agricultural/agronomic consulting, safety consulting, and misc. scientific advisory. Identify the top-3 revenue sub-segments, score the DOMINANT one, and set heterogeneous: true. Best hypothesis, flagged as hypothesis: energy consulting and economic consulting are the largest revenue clusters, with a very large solo-practitioner tail across all niches.

Pricing-model deflation candidate (advisory slice): hourly expert billing invites rescope as AI cuts hours; shared-savings energy deals and seasonal ag retainers are more capture-friendly — model pi_dist by mechanism mix of the dominant segment.
EBITDA normalization: 83% of firms are <5 employees — profit is SDE with owner comp inside; expert practices are extreme key-person businesses, so quoted multiples (where any exist) embed earn-outs.

Boundary confusion: environmental consulting is 541620, engineering is 541330, management consulting is 5416xx siblings, testing labs are 541380, and litigation-support economic consulting straddles 541690/541611 in practice — most named market reports ('energy consulting market') span multiple codes; check definitions.
The record's role_mix is the 4-digit 5416 proxy — treat listed management-analyst dominance as an artifact; rebuild roles from the dominant sub-segment's actual occupations and say so.
